When you’re spending your time, even just an hour, scrolling through things on these social media sites, you just want to feel like you’re getting something real, you know? Something honest. Since I don't have the fancy education to dive deep into digital forensics, I have to rely on common sense, which, given my temperament, is something I need to consciously push myself to use. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It’s a real headache, this whole mess of fake stuff online. When you’re spending your time, even just an hour, scrolling through things on these social media sites, you just want to feel like you’re getting something real, you know? Something honest. With all this nonsense floating around—these fabricated stories or pictures that aren't true—it just adds to the general feeling of being overwhelmed, which, frankly, isn't helping my mood much these days. Since I don't have the fancy education to dive deep into digital forensics, I have to rely on common sense, which, given my temperament, is something I need to consciously push myself to use. My approach, I think, has to be about building a kind of mental filter, a sort of healthy suspicion, without letting that suspicion turn into just outright cynicism. When I see something sensational, something that makes my chest tighten or makes me immediately want to share it because it seems too wild to be true, that’s my first warning bell. Before I let that emotion drive me, I need to pause. I’ve learned, especially when I’m trying to focus on something calmer, like planning a bike ride or looking at a good photograph I took, that rushing into a belief is usually a mistake. I try to check the source, not just the headline. Is it a reputable news outlet, or is it some random account with a profile picture that looks like it was made in MS Paint? If it’s the latter, I treat it like a questionable flyer I picked up on the street—I give it a skeptical look and toss it. Furthermore, I’m trying to curate my feed more deliberately. I’ve started unfollowing accounts that just seem to feed off drama or outrage. I want my little slice of the internet time to be more like reading a decent novel—something with substance—instead of just constant shouting matches. It's a constant battle against the tide of manufactured emotion, and I have to be more conscientious about protecting my own peace of mind from it.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an older man (63), emotionally fragile (depressed, high neuroticism), but outwardly energetic (high extraversion, high conscientiousness). His low agreeableness suggests a tendency toward skepticism or defensiveness. Given his lower secondary education, complex digital analysis is out. His tone must be weary, slightly anxious, but striving for control. The advice needs to be practical, based on common sense, and framed around protecting his mental state (addressing his depression/neuroticism) while maintaining a degree of engagement (fitting his extraversion). The response focuses on developing a 'mental filter' rather than technical debunking.
response_parsed.tone: Weary, Skeptical, Striving for Measured Control
